exemplar.title: Studying the influence of effort in judgements of quality

detailedExemplarData.title: Studying the influence of effort in judgements of quality

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The aim is precise and its relevance is clearly explained by contrasting with Kruger et al. (2004) and linking to everyday decision-making contexts.
The Dual-Processing Model is accurately outlined and directly connected to the investigationâs hypothesis.
Both the IV (4 h vs. 26 h) and DV (price estimate) are correctly operationalized in the directional research hypothesis.
weaknesses: The theory-model connection, while clear, could be deepened by discussing how System II might also influence judgments.
Minor imprecision in the hypothesis wording (e.g., naming levels and measures) prevents a flawless operational statement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets the 5â6 band: the aim is precise, relevance is explained, the theory is described and linked, and variables are operationalized. Minor superficiality in the theory discussion and hypothesis wording justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

strengths: An independent-samples design is not only described but its choice is justified with respect to demand characteristics.
The convenience sampling technique and participant characteristics are detailed and linked to investigative goals.
Controlled variables and materials are identified with some rationale (e.g., unfamiliar painting to avoid bias).
weaknesses: The procedural description is incomplete (cuts off mid-sentence) and hinders reproducibility.
No justification is given for sample size or power, reducing replicability confidence.
The explanation of materials, particularly price-range derivation, is patchy and lacks detail.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While research design and sampling are well explained (band 3â4), major gaps in the procedure and materials rationale limit the overall coherence. This places the criterion at the lower end of the 3â4 band, warranting a 3.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive (median, IQR) and inferential (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics are selected and correctly applied.
The graph addresses the hypothesis with clear labels and condition differentiation.
Findings are interpreted with respect to the data and explicitly linked back to the hypothesis.
weaknesses: Minor reporting inconsistencies (e.g., z-value confusion, means mentioned but not shown in the table).
The graph lacks error bars or dispersion indicators, which would improve clarity.
Full effect-size reporting and exact test statistics (z, r) are omitted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical choices and interpretations are sound, but reporting errors and graphical omissions prevent full band-5 performance. The work fits the 3â4 band, meriting a score of 4.

strengths: Multiple strengths and limitations are stated and clearly explained in terms of their relevance to validity and the hypothesis.
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and are well justified.
Findings are compared to background theory (Kruger et al., System 1 heuristics) rather than simply restated.
weaknesses: The discussion of findings lacks depth (alternative explanations and theoretical nuances are underdeveloped).
The conclusion restates results without fully exploring implications for heuristics or future research.
Some evaluation points remain descriptive rather than analytical.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets aspects of the 5â6 band (strengths/limitations explained, modifications justified) but the superficial depth of the findings discussion places it in the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects this balanced performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart comparing medians is provided, clearly addressing the hypothesis. Axes are labelled, group conditions distinguished, and data values annotated. Nonetheless, the y-axis scale is coarse and there is no error bar or mention of dispersion, constituting minor technical errors. This aligns with band 3-4; a solid but not flawless execution earns 4.

The student states the statistical outcome, interprets it in light of the data (no significant effect; null retained) and explicitly links this to the original hypothesis and to the theoretical expectation. The interpretation is accurate and coherent, satisfying the top-band descriptor. Slight brevity means 5 rather than 6.

Both IV (stated time: 4 h vs 26 h) and DV (price estimate of painting) are clearly identified and operationalised â the IV has two explicit levels and the DV is defined as a multiple-choice price selection. A null and a directional research hypothesis are provided using the operational terms. Wording is precise enough to demonstrate operationalisation; hence the strand sits in the 5-6 band. Minor wording issues (e.g., âhow high or low participants will estimate the paintingâs costâ) prevent full marks, so 5/6 is assigned.

Sampling method (convenience) is identified, participant characteristics are detailed, and the student explains *why* staff were chosen (availability, monetary experience) and acknowledges the limitation of reduced diversity. This constitutes an explanation rather than mere description, justifying band 3-4; the explanation is sound, hence a 4.

The Dual-Processing Model is outlined with descriptions of System 1 and System 2, and the student explicitly links System 1 to the expected heuristic âmore effort = higher valueâ. Although the description is not textbook-level exhaustive, it is accurate and the connection to the present investigation is clearly articulated (âBy applying System Iâ¦ participants wouldâ¦ rely on heuristicsâ). This meets the requirement that the theory is described and its link explained. Minor superficiality keeps the score at 5 rather than 6.

Findings are compared with Kruger et al. (2004) and discussed through the lens of System 1 heuristics, though depth remains limited. The discussion goes beyond mere description but does not fully explore alternative explanations or theoretical nuances. This fits comfortably in the 3-4 band, with quality closer to the upper end, so 4.

The student states a precise aim â to test whether perceived effort (time spent) influences judgements of artistic quality â and explicitly justifies its relevance by contrasting the new staff-member sample with Kruger et al. (2004) and by linking the investigation to decision-making in everyday contexts. The relevance paragraph is brief but clearly goes beyond merely naming relevance; it explains why the question matters for a different demographic. This satisfies the descriptor for the top band, though the explanation lacks great depth, so the lower mark (5) within the 5-6 band is warranted.

Multiple strengths (reduced demand characteristics, individual testing) and limitations (participant variability, convenience bias) are stated and **explained** with clear relevance to validity and the hypothesis. The commentary shows understanding of how each point affects results. Some explanations are brief, so 5/6 is appropriate within the top band.

Controlled variables (quiet room, individual testing, request not to share answers) and materials (specific painting choice, questionnaire design) are provided with some rationale, e.g., selection of an unfamiliar painting to avoid bias. However, explanation of *all* controls is patchy and some claims (e.g., price-range justification) are thin. This still exceeds simple description, so it fits the 3-4 band but merits the lower mark (3).

Proposed modifications (matched-pairs based on art interest, broader sampling, scripted instructions, withholding year of painting) are explicitly tied to previously identified limitations and justified in terms of improved control or generalisability. The explicit linkage and justification meet the 5-6 band; the reasoning, while solid, lacks the comprehensive detail for a 6, so a mark of 5 is assigned.

The student not only describes using an independent-samples design but also justifies its choice by referring to demand characteristics and impossibility of repeated exposure. This explanatory rationale places the work in the 3-4 band; the explanation is coherent, so the top of the band (4) is appropriate.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, median, IQR) and inferential (one-tailed Mann-Whitney U) statistics are chosen correctly for ordinal, independent data. Calculations appear accurate and the decision regarding significance is correct. Minor reporting issues (confusing z value) prevent a flawless presentation, so 5/6 is awarded within the highest band.

annotations.comment: The aim statement clearly specifies investigating effort and time spent in art quality judgments, establishing a focused research question. Well done linking effort directly to the cognitive process under study.
Although the Dual Processing Model is described accurately, the connection to the specific variables could be deepened by explicitly stating how System II might also influence price judgments.
The paragraph ends abruptly (âThis study willâ), indicating a missing sentence. Complete the explanation of hypotheses here for clarity and flow.
Relevance is well justified by highlighting a new demographic (staff vs. art enthusiasts) and practical decision-making implications. This effectively explains why the investigation matters.
The list bullets identify IV and DV but lack operational precision. Rephrase the hypothesis in a single statement specifying levels (4 h vs. 26 h) and price estimate scale.
The null hypothesis is stated correctly but could be improved by explicitly naming the IV and DV to mirror the research hypothesis format.
The research hypothesis is directional and operationalises the IV levels and DV estimates effectively. Good alignment with a one-tailed prediction.
Your design rationale explains avoiding demand characteristics effectively, but clarify what is meant by âinternalize the differing hoursâ to strengthen reader understanding.
Modification suggestion to implement a matched-pairs design is well linked to limitation of art knowledge variability. Excellent justification.
Sampling modification to include other schools is relevant; strengthen by specifying how you would recruit and balance demographics.
The DV bullet includes a stray symbol (â ). Ensure mathematical notation is clear or replace with descriptive text.
The procedure description cuts off mid-sentence (âshowing theâ). Complete this to ensure the steps are fully traceable.
Convenience sampling is described and the demographic justified. Consider adding how sample size (n=20) was determined for adequate power.
Participant characteristics are detailed (gender balance, art familiarity). Good explanation of choice; could mention how age diversity supports generalizability.
Ethical procedures (consent, debrief, anonymity) are thorough and align with best practice. This strengthens the studyâs validity.
Controls are clearly outlined and justified (empty room, no sharing). Well-linked to internal validity concerns.
The materials rationale explains painting choice to avoid prior bias. Consider adding how price options were derived statistically (e.g., Â± X%).
The summary table correctly presents median and IQR by condition. However, report sample size (n=10) and mean if mentioned in the text.
Text describes mean values not shown in the table; this inconsistency should be resolved by either adding mean in the table or removing it from text.
The bar chart effectively visualizes median differences and addresses the hypothesis, but error bars or boxplots would better illustrate dispersion.
Selection of a one-tailed MannâWhitney U test is appropriate given the directional hypothesis and ordinal data. Good statistical choice.
The test result (U=49, p=0.48405) is interpreted correctly. For fuller reporting, include the exact z-score and effect size.
The evaluation summary restates results but lacks deeper discussion of why System 1 heuristics may not have emerged in staff participants.
The discussion contrasts findings with Kruger et al. (2004) but would benefit from examining alternative explanations or demographic effects.
Strengths and limitations are identified under âDesignâ but could be better separated and explicitly linked to hypothesis validity.
Procedure strength: individual sessions reduce social influence. Consider scripting all instructions to avoid leading-word variations.
The conclusion restates medians and null outcome but should include theoretical implications for heuristics and future research directions.
The modification to withhold painting year is well justified; consider also controlling for other contextual cues like perceived prestige.
